Grief does not exempt power from audit.

The strongest criticisms of Ali Khamenei are not dramatic. They are structural.

He presided over a system where:

Dissent was securitised.
Pluralism was filtered.
Authority was vertically consolidated.

When protest becomes a threat by definition, civic oxygen thins. That is not opinion — it is governance mechanics.

Civil liberties operated within ideological boundaries.
Oversight mechanisms flowed upward, not outward.
Correction was internal, not reciprocal.

Supporters call this stability.
Critics call it repression.

Ethically, the concern is straightforward:

When power centralises without robust external constraint, accountability narrows.
When dissent is framed as destabilisation, autonomy becomes conditional.
When regional influence is pursued through armed networks, proportionality becomes morally complex.

No theatrics required.

Leadership can be durable and ethically restrictive at the same time.

Death closes biography.
It does not close evaluation.

SWANK London
We assess architecture, not atmospheres.

text:
If President Donald Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu authorized strikes against Iran absent a demonstrable, imminent armed attack or authorization from the United Nations Security Council, then this was not defensive necessity — it was a calculated act of unilateral escalation.

This decision cannot be obscured behind rhetoric about deterrence or existential threat without publicly verifiable evidence meeting the legal threshold established by the Charter of the United Nations. Article 2(4) is explicit: the use of force is prohibited except under narrow, defined exceptions. Expanding those exceptions through executive interpretation does not strengthen international security; it weakens the rule itself.

Preventive war doctrine — striking because a state might pose a threat in the future — is not codified international law. It is strategic preference masquerading as necessity.

By bypassing multilateral verification frameworks and established diplomatic channels, U.S. and Israeli leadership signal that institutional oversight is subordinate to unilateral judgment. That message reverberates globally. If powerful states reserve the right to determine which governments are intolerable and strike accordingly, then the prohibition on force becomes conditional on power asymmetry.

This is not a theoretical concern. It is structural destabilization.

The consequences are foreseeable:

Increased probability of retaliatory strikes

Activation of regional proxy networks

Maritime disruption and energy shock

Expanded military confrontation beyond the initial theater

Each of these outcomes becomes more likely when force is used without collective authorization.

There is also a moral hazard embedded in the messaging. Encouraging internal opposition movements in Iran while escalating military pressure exposes civilians to heightened repression. When foreign leaders amplify calls for political change without possessing the capacity — or intention — to physically protect those who respond, they transfer risk downward. The cost is borne by ordinary citizens, not decision-makers in Washington or Jerusalem.

Accountability must therefore be explicit.

If this action proves to have been outside the bounds of lawful self-defense, then it constitutes a breach not only of legal norms but of strategic prudence. It places regional stability at risk in pursuit of objectives that have not been transparently justified to the international community.

History does not judge leaders solely by whether they acted forcefully. It judges whether they acted responsibly within the constraints of law and with full awareness of systemic consequences.

If law is selectively applied, then it ceases to function as law.

If power becomes the final arbiter, then global order becomes provisional.

And if escalation unfolds from this decision, the responsibility will not be diffuse. It will trace directly to the political leadership that chose unilateral force over multilateral constraint.

War with Iran would be a military, political and economic disaster.
Yes, Iran is currently engaged in asymmetric conflict through surrogates like the Houthi. This is a continuing campaign to force the US out of the region through attrition and exhaustion. This type of warfare can and is being countered through support for US surrogates and "black ops" or through mitigation strategies that reduce or eliminate the impacts of Iran's strategies.
Yes, the US has the superiority in air and maritime power to eliminate most of Iran's air defense static infrastructure and its maritime and most of its coastal defense forces. This would allow for the isolation of Iran's access to the sea and, primarily its Chinese "partner". It would not, however, isolate Iran from selling and moving oil and other natural resources through land transportation nodes unless the US was willing to engage in an expensive air campaign with significant collateral damage possibilities.
But there are constraints on such a campaign, constraints that would be even more impactful on any sort of ground campaign. The Saudis have made it plain that their military, air and naval bases are not available to a US only operation that is not in response to direct Iranian military operations against the Saudis or their allies. What is more, the Trump Administration's reckless application of economic warfare against most of the world and even our closest allies, has left the US with few friends and fewer who would provide any assistance in a war with Iran. In Iraq in 2003, we could depend on the Brits and ANZACs. In Afghanistan in 2002, NATO allies like France, Canada, the Netherlands and Germany provided ground troops. Any ops, air, naval or ground conducted against the Iranians would be unilateral and without political, economic or political support from previously dependable allies thanks to Trump and his merry band of sycophantic incompetents like Rubio and Hegseth.
Many years ago I was involved in theater planning at the CENTCOM and ARCENT level for ground operations against Iran and Syria after the seemingly effortless victory against Iraq in 1991. Planners had to be frequently reminded of the disadvantageous terrain behind the only two viable ocean ports, Bander Khomeini and Bander Abbas. They had to be reminded of the Iranian domestic political situation and the cultural and social values that created the willingness to accept casualties and hardship by the Iranian armed forces and para-military during the Iran-Iraq War, 1979-1989. There was the political, cultural, social and religious issues that would be activated by an American attempt at regime change which would require the occupation for at least two generations. Force levels in the 700K to 1M US and US-equivalent troops range would be necessary to disarm the armed forces, the regime's political para-military and militias and keep the lid on any insurgency in response to occupation. Casualties during the conventional force operations would approximate WW2 or Korean Conflict numbers and continued attrition near that suffered by US forces in Vietnam counter-insurgency ops.  
This is not the time, politically and economically to get involved in serious conventional military ops which would see defense spending go from 4.5% equivalent of the GDP to 8-9% of the Vietnam Conflict or even the 15% of the Korean Conflict. So it isn’t an order.
It’s not a memo.
It’s forgetting by saturation.

And it works every time.

Allah also says:

“When the hypocrites come to you, they say, ‘We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah.’ … And Allah testifies that the hypocrites are liars.”

The Qur’an reminds us that power without justice becomes tyranny. Leaders — whether in the East or the West — are accountable before Allah. No president, prime minister, or ruler is above divine justice.
Allah commands:
“O you who believe! Stand firmly for justice, as witnesses for Allah, even if it is against yourselves or parents and relatives…”
(Surah An-Nisa 4:135)
This means we must criticize injustice consistently — not based on nationality, race, or political party — but based on truth and justice.

The Qur’an teaches us:
“And do not let the hatred of a people prevent you from being just. Be just; that is nearer to righteousness.”
(Surah Al-Ma’idah 5:8)
True reform begins with justice, integrity, and taqwa — not slogans.

We live in a time that feels deeply aligned with the warnings of the Qur’an about فساد (corruption) and hypocrisy.
Allah says in Qur'an:
“And when it is said to them, ‘Do not cause corruption on the earth,’ they say, ‘We are but reformers.’

(Surah Al-Baqarah 2:11–12)
How relevant this feels today. Many claim to be peacemakers, unifiers, defenders of democracy, protectors of security — yet their policies ignite wars, deepen injustice, and divide humanity.
Allah also says:
“Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what the hands of people have earned…”
(Surah Ar-Rum 30:41)
And about hypocrisy in leadership:
“When the hypocrites come to you, they say, ‘We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah.’ … And Allah testifies that the hypocrites are liars.”
(Surah Al-Munafiqun 63:1)
The Qur’an reminds us that power without justice becomes tyranny. Leaders — whether in the East or the West — are accountable before Allah. No president, prime minister, or ruler is above divine justice.
Allah commands:
“O you who believe! Stand firmly for justice, as witnesses for Allah, even if it is against yourselves or parents and relatives…”
(Surah An-Nisa 4:135)
This means we must criticize injustice consistently — not based on nationality, race, or political party — but based on truth and justice.
If leaders like Donald Trump, Benjamin Netanyahu, or any other political figure pursue policies that harm civilians, spread oppression, or fuel conflict, then those actions deserve moral scrutiny and accountability. But our criticism should reflect Qur’anic character — firm, truthful, and principled — not abusive.
The Qur’an teaches us:
“And do not let the hatred of a people prevent you from being just. Be just; that is nearer to righteousness.”
(Surah Al-Ma’idah 5:8)
True reform begins with justice, integrity, and taqwa — not slogans.
In the end, every ruler will stand before Allah alone.
And history always testifies.

3. The reliance on FDI (Foriegn Direct Investment) is a thing of its past. Back in the 1980s, China could not even make a proper golf club. FDI was very important to them. Today, the self reliance is the main drive. Also I looked at the FDI numbers (I am one of the FDIs) not that they are not coming in, they are coming in in a smaller scale. Perhaps in the old days there were more companies coming in, but today there are not as many. However, China stop relying on FDI for some time. 4. What Trump does, are 2 things: (a) Reassure China must pump up its own sector and confirming their will not to rely on USA any more (look at that 9% import from US versus, what 20% plus) (b) Trump's action is weakening USA themselves - not that it's good for the world but just a part of history that US must walk thru and emerge as something better. Trump would become a wake up call to the Americans - not because what he is, is a reminder to all Americans what not to be. This change will take time for the Americans to realise, it will be 2-3 generation thing. During this period of time, China will fix themselves. What Trump is doing now, is similar to the end of Ming Dynasty when China was super arrogant. They paid their price towards the end of the next dynasty - the Qing dynasty. Like I said, the people in US today will not suffer. It's their kids and grand kids will pay the price that Trump is making today. You need not to worry about it. You are living off the greatness of the American era prepared by Reagan / Eisenhower for your guys. You are enjoying the fruits that was set by these great Americans. People like us look them up to. We belong to the bunch who will go into our own graveyard very soon, so it does not matter for us now, but if you do care for your next generation, I think you should think very hard what you should be doing next.
